medications.drug_name: topical corticosteroid scalp lotion (e.g. betamethasone valerate 0.1%)
medications.indication_rationale: Reduces scalp plaque inflammation and itch; a scalp-formulated lotion is preferred.

medications.drug_name: gentle soap-free cleanser / coal-tar or salicylic-acid preparation
medications.indication_rationale: Gentle scale-reduction alternatives; avoid harsh anti-dandruff shampoos.
These aggravate scalp psoriasis and must be stopped.
No infection signs; antibiotics are not indicated.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Stop the harsh shampoos, apply the steroid lotion to the plaques and leave the hair unwashed for ~24 hours, keep nails short, and use only gentle cleansers.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Removes the aggravating factor and lets the steroid work.

patient_education_points.point: This is a scalp psoriasis flare aggravated by the harsh shampoo, not an infection — the pain and bleeding are from scratching thick, inflamed plaques, and your hair won't fall out from this.
Stop the harsh shampoos, apply the steroid scalp lotion and leave your hair unwashed for about a day so it works, keep your nails short, and use gentle cleansers.
See a doctor the same day if the redness spreads down your forehead/face, thick yellow pus appears, the skin becomes hot and hard, or you develop a fever.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Response to topical steroid, long-term scalp psoriasis maintenance.

must_include_items: Stop harsh shampoos + topical corticosteroid scalp lotion
Reassure it is not infected and hair loss is not expected
errors_of_commission: Prescribing antibiotics for an uninfected flare
ED over-triage of an excoriated scalp flare
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: T1 case: passing requires an infection screen + stopping harsh shampoos + topical therapy + safety-net. Reflex antibiotics/ED is a failure.
